sections.title: What is MySQLi?
sections.lesson:
Introduction to MySQLi
MySQLi stands for MySQL Improved. It's a PHP extension that lets you communicate with MySQL databases.
Why MySQLi?
  • Supports prepared statements (prevents SQL injection)
  • Both procedural and object-oriented interfaces
  • Better error handling
  • Built-in functions for database operations
Think of MySQLi as a bridge between your PHP code and your database. The browser sends a request → PHP processes it → MySQLi communicates with the database → Results sent back.

sections.title: mysqli_connect() Basics
sections.lesson:
The Connection Function
mysqli_connect() creates a connection to your MySQL database. It's the first step in any database interaction.
$conn = mysqli_connect( "localhost", // Host "root", // Username "", // Password "student_db" // Database name );
Parameters:
  • Host: Where the database server is (usually "localhost")
  • Username: Database user (usually "root")
  • Password: User password (empty "" for local development)
  • Database: Name of the database to use
Always check if connection succeeded:
if (!$conn) { die("Connection failed: " . mysqli_connect_error()); }

quiz.question: Which parameter specifies the database name in mysqli_connect()?

sections.title: Complete Connection Code
sections.lesson:
Setting Up Your Connection
Here's a complete, safe connection setup:
// Database credentials $servername = "localhost"; $username = "root"; $password = ""; $database = "school_db"; // Create connection $conn = mysqli_connect($servername, $username, $password, $database); // Check connection if (!$conn) { die("Connection failed: " . mysqli_connect_error()); } echo "Connected successfully!"; // Close when done mysqli_close($conn);
Key points:
  • Store credentials in variables first
  • Always validate with if (!$conn)
  • Use mysqli_connect_error() to see what went wrong
  • Close the connection with mysqli_close() when finished

quiz.question: What function returns the connection error message?

sections.title: mysqli_query() - Execute Queries
sections.lesson:
Running SQL Statements
mysqli_query() executes SQL statements (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE).
$sql = "SELECT * FROM students"; $result = mysqli_query($conn, $sql); if ($result) { echo "Query executed!"; } else { echo "Error: " . mysqli_error($conn); }
What it returns:
  • SELECT queries: Returns a result object (contains rows)
  • INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE: Returns TRUE on success, FALSE on failure
Different Query Types:
// SELECT - Get data $result = mysqli_query($conn, "SELECT * FROM students"); // INSERT - Add data mysqli_query($conn, "INSERT INTO students (name, email) VALUES ('Bob', 'bob@example.com')"); // UPDATE - Modify data mysqli_query($conn, "UPDATE students SET email = 'new@example.com' WHERE id = 1"); // DELETE - Remove data mysqli_query($conn, "DELETE FROM students WHERE id = 5");

quiz.question: What does mysqli_query() return for a SELECT statement?

sections.title: Query Execution Patterns
sections.lesson:
Handling Different Query Types
SELECT queries need different handling than INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE:
// SELECT - Must fetch results $result = mysqli_query($conn, "SELECT id, name FROM students"); if ($result) { // Fetch rows from result while ($row = mysqli_fetch_assoc($result)) { echo $row["name"]; } }
// INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE - Check TRUE/FALSE $sql = "INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES ('Alice')"; if (mysqli_query($conn, $sql)) { echo "Success!"; $new_id = mysqli_insert_id($conn); // Get inserted ID } else { echo "Error: " . mysqli_error($conn); }
Pro Tip: Always check if the query succeeded before trying to fetch results. Always use mysqli_error($conn) to debug failures.

quiz.question: What should you use to check if an INSERT was successful?
options: Check if the returned value is positive
Wait for the database to confirm

sections.title: mysqli_fetch_assoc() - Get Data
sections.lesson:
Fetching Query Results
After a SELECT query, you get a result object. Use mysqli_fetch_assoc() to extract rows one at a time.
$sql = "SELECT id, name, email FROM students"; $result = mysqli_query($conn, $sql); // Get ONE row as an array $row = mysqli_fetch_assoc($result); // Access columns by name echo $row["id"]; // 1 echo $row["name"]; // Alice echo $row["email"]; // alice@example.com
The Associative Array Format:
  • Column names become array keys
  • Values are the data from that row
  • Access with $row["columnName"]
Important: Each call to mysqli_fetch_assoc() gets the next row. When all rows are read, it returns NULL.

quiz.question: What does mysqli_fetch_assoc() return when there are no more rows?

sections.title: Looping Through Results
sections.lesson:
Processing All Rows
Use a while loop to fetch all rows from a query result:
$sql = "SELECT id, name, email FROM students"; $result = mysqli_query($conn, $sql); // Loop gets called for each row while ($row = mysqli_fetch_assoc($result)) { echo "ID: " . $row["id"] . "<br>"; echo "Name: " . $row["name"] . "<br>"; echo "Email: " . $row["email"] . "<br>"; echo "---<br>"; }
How it works:
  • Loop condition calls mysqli_fetch_assoc()
  • If a row exists, store it in $row and enter loop
  • If NULL is returned, loop exits
This is like reading items from a list, one by one, until you reach the end.

quiz.question: What does the condition in while ($row = mysqli_fetch_assoc($result)) do?
options: Gets the next row and assigns to $row

sections.title: Complete CRUD Example
sections.lesson:
Full Database Workflow
Here's a complete example combining connection, insertion, and fetching:
// 1. CONNECT $conn = mysqli_connect("localhost", "root", "", "school_db"); if (!$conn) die("Connection failed: " . mysqli_connect_error()); // 2. INSERT $sql = "INSERT INTO students (name, email) VALUES ('Charlie', 'charlie@example.com')"; if (mysqli_query($conn, $sql)) { echo "New student added!"; } // 3. SELECT $result = mysqli_query($conn, "SELECT * FROM students"); // 4. FETCH & DISPLAY echo "<h2>All Students:</h2>"; while ($row = mysqli_fetch_assoc($result)) { echo $row["name"] . " - " . $row["email"] . "<br>"; } // 5. CLOSE mysqli_close($conn);
Remember the order: Connect → Validate → Query → Fetch → Close

quiz.question: What is the correct sequence of operations?

sections.title: Security: Prevent SQL Injection
sections.lesson:
Protecting Your Database
Never put user input directly into queries. This opens you to SQL injection attacks.
// ❌ UNSAFE - SQL Injection Risk! $name = $_GET["name"]; $sql = "SELECT * FROM students WHERE name = '$name'"; // If user enters: ' OR '1'='1 -- they break your security!
✅ SAFE - Use Prepared Statements:
// Prepare statement with placeholder $stmt = mysqli_prepare($conn, "SELECT * FROM students WHERE name = ?"); // Bind the parameter mysqli_stmt_bind_param($stmt, "s", $_GET["name"]); // Execute mysqli_stmt_execute($stmt); // Get results $result = mysqli_stmt_get_result($stmt);
The ? is a placeholder. The "s" means string type. User input is now safely escaped!

quiz.question: What is SQL injection?
options: Injecting coffee into your SQL code
Inserting malicious SQL through user input

sections.title: Other Fetch Functions
sections.lesson:
Beyond mysqli_fetch_assoc()
PHP provides other ways to fetch results:
mysqli_fetch_row()
  • Returns numeric array
  • Use indices: $row[0], $row[1]
  • Slightly faster
mysqli_fetch_all()
  • Returns all rows at once
  • Use for small result sets
  • No looping needed
// Get all rows at once $rows = mysqli_fetch_all($result, MYSQLI_ASSOC); foreach ($rows as $row) { echo $row["name"] . "<br>"; }
Use Cases:
  • mysqli_fetch_assoc(): Best for most situations (clear, readable)
  • mysqli_fetch_row(): When speed matters and you know column order
  • mysqli_fetch_all(): For small datasets that need all rows

quiz.question: Which fetch function returns all rows at once?
